steps.title: Setting the Stage
narrative: Warm, humid air was spreading north while a strong weather system approached the Plains.
This is the big-picture setup that helped prepare the atmosphere for severe storms later in the day.

1.title: Setting the Stage
narrative: Start by finding the main fronts and the overall storm setup.
Notice where the warm, unstable air is located compared to the boundaries.

1.title: What Forecasters Watch
narrative: Surface fronts often help focus storm development.
This is one reason the setup matters before radar gets busy.

1.title: Why This Matters
narrative: A severe weather day usually begins with a favorable large-scale pattern.
This map helps explain why later warnings did not happen by surprise.

1.title: What Comes Next
narrative: The next step is to compare the setup with the severe weather outlook.
That helps connect the forecast to the actual environment.

steps.title: Forecasters Saw the Risk Early
narrative: This region was highlighted for severe weather before storms fully developed.
Forecast outlooks help show where the atmosphere is most supportive of dangerous storms.

1.title: Forecasters Saw the Risk Early
narrative: Notice how the highlighted risk area covers eastern South Dakota and nearby states.
This shows that severe weather potential was recognized ahead of time.

1.title: Forecast Thinking
narrative: SPC outlooks are designed to communicate where dangerous storms are most likely.
They are one of the first clues in the severe weather story.

1.title: Forecast to Reality
narrative: The outlook is not radar yet, but it sets expectations for the day.
It gives a forecast framework before storms intensify.

1.title: What Comes Next
narrative: Now it is time to look at the air mass itself.
A dangerous setup becomes more meaningful when moisture and instability increase.

steps.title: The Atmosphere Becomes Dangerous
narrative: Moisture was increasing across the region as the afternoon continued.
That richer low-level air helped create a more favorable environment for strong storms.

1.title: The Atmosphere Becomes Dangerous
narrative: This view highlights dew point so moisture is easier to see and explain.
Watch where the richer air is spreading and pooling.

1.title: Why Moisture Matters
narrative: Warm, moist air is one of the key ingredients for severe thunderstorms.
It helps support stronger updrafts and more energetic storm development.

1.title: A Simple Science View
narrative: This is one way to show that the atmosphere was becoming more storm-ready.
The setup and the air mass were lining up in the same region.

1.title: What Comes Next
narrative: Once storms form in this kind of environment, radar trends become much more important.
That is where the story turns from setup to action.

steps.title: Now the Storm Comes Alive
narrative: Radar showed a fast-moving line of severe storms racing across the region.
As the system intensified, warnings were issued and reports of damage began to appear.

1.title: Now the Storm Comes Alive
narrative: This is the most important scene in the public demo.
Watch how radar, warnings, and reports begin to work together.

1.title: A Real-Time Feel
narrative: Radar shows where the storm is, warnings show where danger is being communicated, and reports show what people are experiencing.
Together they create a more complete picture of the event.

1.title: Why This Is Powerful
narrative: This step brings forecasting, observation, and impact together in one view.
It shows why an interactive simulator can teach more than a static image.

1.title: What Comes Next
narrative: The final step connects the storm to the real world on the ground.
That is where reports and photos help tell the human side of the story.

steps.title: Real Impact on the Ground
narrative: Storm reports help connect the weather map to what was happening in towns and counties on the ground.
This final step shows that the derecho was not just a radar event — it had real impact.

1.title: Real Impact on the Ground
narrative: Watch where reports cluster compared to the storm path.
This helps connect the map to real-world damage and eyewitness accounts.

1.title: Why Reports Matter
narrative: Storm reports add ground truth to radar and warning information.
They help confirm what kind of damage or severe weather actually occurred.

1.title: Human Impact
narrative: A weather event becomes more meaningful when people can connect science to lived experience.
That is one reason photos and reports are so valuable in education.

1.title: Where the Full Lab Goes Further
narrative: This demo is only a first look.
The full lab experience can go deeper into observations, analysis, and scientific investigation.
